coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots using quince fruit (Cydonia oblonga) powder as carbon precursor: Application in cell imaging and As3+ determination
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Ramezani, Zahra
Qorbanpour, Maryam
Rahbar, Nadereh
coredata.dc:description: High stable and luminescent multicolor carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared using quince fruit (Cydonia oblonga) powder as carbon precursors in one pot via microwave irradiation. The Taguchi orthogonal array using four factors at three levels was applied to obtain CQDs with high photoluminescent intensity. Taguchi ranking results revealed that temperature and microwave power has the most and the least effect on the CQDs photoluminescence intensities, respectively. Prepared CQDs were characterized using different microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. The results reveal that average size of the prepared particles was 4.85 ± 0.07 nm. The CQDs have maximum emission intensity at 450 nm if excited at 350 nm with a quantum yield of 8.55%. CQDs prepared via microwave in 30 min were compared with those synthesized hydrothermally in a Teflon-linen stainless steel body autoclave at 200 °C. High performance liquid chromatographic / photodiode array detector evaluation of the CQDs reveals different surface functionality for the carbon dots. Fe3+ among cations and MnO4 − among the anions, strongly extinguish the photoluminescence at 450 nm. Oxidation of As3+ by MnO4 − became the basis for arsenic assay in aqueous solutions. Addition of As3+ to MnO4 − before mixing with CQDs, compare to permanganate alone, increases the photoluminescence. This increase has a linear relation with As3+ concentration, in the range of 0.1–2 μg mL−1. Finally, it revealed that the CQDs are not cytotoxic for HT-29 cell line and it is possible to use them in cell imaging and drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Arsenic detection
Green microwave synthesis
Quince fruit
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CQDs synthesis using quince fruit as carbon precursors
Application of CQDs in As3+ sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM image (a), TEM (b), and PSA (c) for CQDs prepared by microwave-assisted synthesis using quince fruit powder as a carbon precursor.
FTIR (a), and XRD (b); XPS C1S (c), O1s (d), N1s (e), and S2p (f) of CQDs prepared by microwave-assisted synthesis.

UV/Vis absorption spectra of microwave synthesized CQDs. (a) Inset is the as-prepared particles under daylight (left) and 360 nm in UV cabinet (right).; and excitation and emission spectra for the CQDs at 450 nm emission and 350 nm excitation, respectively (b); PL spectra of microwave synthesis of CQDs using quince fruit as carbon precursor at optimized conditions mentioned in the main manuscript (c, d). Excitation wavelengths are identified in the figures.

The CQDs deposited on the microscopic lam and observed by invert fluorescence microscopic at different excitation. Excitation filters are, SW = 420–480 nm, WB = 400–440 nm, WIB = 460-495 nm, WU = 330–385 nm, and WIG = 530–550 nm.

Chromatogram for high-performance liquid chromatography analysis of CQDs prepared by microwave heating at 254 nm with A) MeOH: H2O 50:50) and B) ACN: H2O (50:50) as the mobile phase. UV/Vis spectra at each retention time are shown in the right side.

Fluorescent image of the HT-29 cell after incubation with 1000 μg mL−1 microwave synthesized CQDs for a) 4 h (excitation and emission filters were 488 nm and 518 nm, consequently); b) Cytotoxicity of CQDs for of HT-29 cell determined by MTT assay.
Figure of merits of the present method for determination of As3+.
Determination of added As3+ to tap water using standard and matrix calibration curve.

row.entry: Matrix calibration a Found (μg mL−1) Recovery%

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calibration curve was obtained in tap water.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots using quince fruit (Cydonia oblonga) powder as carbon precursor: Application in cell imaging and As3+ determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences, Ahvaz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Student Research Committee, Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences, Ahvaz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High stable and luminescent multicolor carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared using quince fruit (Cydonia oblonga) powder as carbon precursors in one pot via microwave irradiation. The Taguchi orthogonal array using four factors at three levels was applied to obtain CQDs with high photoluminescent intensity. Taguchi ranking results revealed that temperature and microwave power has the most and the least effect on the CQDs photoluminescence intensities, respectively. Prepared CQDs were characterized using different microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. The results reveal that average size of the prepared particles was 4.85 ± 0.07 nm. The CQDs have maximum emission intensity at 450 nm if excited at 350 nm with a quantum yield of 8.55%. CQDs prepared via microwave in 30 min were compared with those synthesized hydrothermally in a Teflon-linen stainless steel body autoclave at 200 °C. High performance liquid chromatographic / photodiode array detector evaluation of the CQDs reveals different surface functionality for the carbon dots. Fe3+ among cations and MnO4 − among the anions, strongly extinguish the photoluminescence at 450 nm. Oxidation of As3+ by MnO4 − became the basis for arsenic assay in aqueous solutions. Addition of As3+ to MnO4 − before mixing with CQDs, compare to permanganate alone, increases the photoluminescence. This increase has a linear relation with As3+ concentration, in the range of 0.1–2 μg mL−1. Finally, it revealed that the CQDs are not cytotoxic for HT-29 cell line and it is possible to use them in cell imaging and drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: There has been an increasing interest in carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a new class of carbon nanoparticles since its accidental discovery in 2004. This interest is attributed to its high water solubility, biocompatibility due to their nanometer dimension and low toxicity, and specific physical and chemical properties [1]. By surface modification of CQDs, they successfully used as chemical and biological sensing devices [2–5], drug delivery vehicles for the combination of medical therapy and bio-imaging to visualize drug distribution at a target tissue [6–10], and pollutant remediation [11].

CQDs were synthesized through different protocols such as top-down method of chemical ablation, electrochemical carbonization, laser ablation, microwave irradiation, and hydrothermal/solvothermal treatments [12,13]. However, microwave heating appears to be effective compared to conventional heating because microwave irradiation provides energy-efficient internal heating by direct coupling of the microwave energy with dipoles and ions present in the reaction mixture. This direct interaction results in fast heating rates not reproducible with conventional heating [14,15]. In the early days of microwave synthesis, household microwave ovens has been used for chemical synthesis. Although nowadays dedicated instrumentation is available, there are still researchers who use kitchen microwave ovens for scientific purposes [16]. The scientific microwave has many advantages over domestic microwave ovens such as improving reproducibility via temperature and microwave power control, the possibility of stirring the solution during microwave irradiation, a higher level of safety, as well as convenient programing and data management. They also reduce the amount of the reactants and the reaction time [17].

A vast variety of carbon precursors has been introduced for CQDs’ synthesis, but most of them require organic chemicals and solvents for engineering the CQDs’ surface. One-pot green synthesis without hazardous solvents or chemicals has attracted the attention of numerous scientists. Natural sources, due to the presence of multicomponent, can produce particles with different surface defects and highly luminescence. Further, advantages such as ease of preparation and being cost effective encouraged scientists to introduce different natural carbon source in CQDs synthesis for a variety of applications. In recent research tomatoes, [18] peanut shells, [19] citrus peel, [20] bio-waste, [21] cellulose, [22] and egg yolk [23] have been employed as a carbon source, and CQDs prepared for certain applications. Simultaneous CQDs’ synthesis, functionalization, and passivation with no added chemicals or organic solvents are performed by using these carbon sources.

In the current study, stable and high luminescent CQDs are synthesized from quince fruit with no additional chemicals. Microwave heating was selected because of high speed and lower amount of the starting materials compared to the hydrothermal methods. Taguchi orthogonal array was used to optimize the parameters in syntheses such as the amount of quince fruit powder, microwave power, temperature, and irradiation time. CQDs prepared at optimized conditions were compared with its hydrothermal counterparts. The possible application of the particles in cell imaging was investigated by its HT-29 cell uptake and the cell viability by MTT assay. Arsenic is among the most important environmental pollutants. As3+ is the most toxic arsenic species [24], and its detection and determination are of vital importance. Therefore, a fluorimetric sensor for As3+ determination in aqueous solutions was introduced, based on As3+ oxidation by permanganate ion. It was shown that a fixed amount of permanganate mixed with different As3+ concentration could increase fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-MnO4 −, and detection of As3+ becomes possible.

Silver nitrate, potassium dichromate, potassium permanganate, sodium oxalate, potassium iodide, potassium chloride, phosphate salts, citric acid, hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, ferrous chloride, and standard solution (1000 μg mL−1) of other cations were all purchased from Merck (Darmaschtat, Germany). HPLC grade Methanol (MeOH) and acetonitrile (ACN) were acquired from Merck (Darmaschtat, Germany). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 medium were obtained from Gibco (USA). Penicillin-streptomycin, 3-(4, 5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). HT-29 (human colorectal adenocarcinoma) cell lines were obtained from National Cell Bank of Iran (NCBI, Pastor Institute of Iran). Double-distilled water was used wherever required. Quince fruit (Cydonia oblonga) was purchased from Ahvaz market during January 2017 and washed thoroughly with tap and distilled water, consequently. Then they were cut into small pieces and lyophilized. Quince powder was passed through sieves with mesh size 100, and uniform particles (149 μm) were separated and kept in a cool, dry, and dark place for future use. 3KD centrifuge filter units (Merck Millipore Ltd., Tullagreen, Carrigtwohill, Co. Cork, Ireland) were used for purification of CQDs.

Transmission electron microscopic image (TEM) was recorded on Zeiss-EM10C electron microscope (Germany) after dispersing on carbon-coated copper grid at 80 KV. CQDs were dispersed in acetone and one drop of the CQDs suspension was placed on mica sheet and particles were viewed under A JPK (Germany) atomic force microscope (AFM). Scatteroscope I, Qudix (Korea) and Malvern zeta sizer was used for particle size analysis (PSA) and zeta potential determination. Olympus IX71 fluorescent microscope (Japan) was used to observe emission of the CQDs deposited on the microscope lams at different excitation and its intracellular uptake in the cells. A Vortex 70 FTIR (Brucker; Germany) was utilized to determine the functional group on the CQDs. A Photon X Ar 201 (Iran) UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to record UV spectra. Thermo scientific luminescence spectrometer (USA) was used to record luminescence spectra. ELISA plate reader (BioRad, USA) were used for MTT assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs synthesis using quince fruit as carbon precursors

ce:section.ce:para: Taguchi orthogonal array using four factors at three levels were used for microwave synthesis of CQDs using quince fruit as carbon precursor and distilled water as solvent. Table S1 (see electronic supplementary information (ESI)) lists the factors and their corresponding levels. Optimum value determined by analyzing the experiment results by Minitab15 are presented in Table S2 and Fig. S1 (see ESI). CQDs were synthesized at optimal conditions for further studies. To describe, 0.1 g quince powder and 25 ml distilled water were added to Teflon microwave vessel and optical fiber sensor was inserted into the vessel. The vessel was tightly sealed, placed in microwave oven, and temperature of the reaction was risen to 220 °C in 1 min and 30 s using 850 W microwave irradiations. Reactions proceeded at this temperature for 30 min through controlling the duration of microwave irradiation (700 W). Fig. S2 (ESI) shows the whole microwave program applied at optimal conditions. After cooling, brown solution was centrifuged to separate large particles. Finally, brown solution was purified using 3KD centrifugal filter unit. The particles were freeze-dried and the resulting powder was used for further studies.

About 0.4 g quince fruit powder was mixed with 40 ml distilled water and transferred to Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Autoclave were closed tightly and heated at 200 °C for 4 h in furnace. After cooling, the resulting solution was centrifuged (900 rpm) for separation of brown solution from large and unreacted particles. Freeze dried CQDs were used to compare with those prepared through microwave heating.

Quinone sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid has quantum yield 0.54 (54%) excited at 350 nm. It was used as standard for quantum yield determination by Eq. (1)
(1)
Φ u
=
Φ s
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
η u
η s
Where, Φs and Φu stand for quantum yield of standard and CQDs, consequently. F, A, η stand for fluorescence intensity, absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and refractive index, respectively. Solutions of quinone sulfate and CQDs having absorbance below 0.05 were excited at 350 nm, and their emission at 450 nm was recorded. Then, the quantum yield was determined by replacing the corresponding values in Eq 1.

Agilent Technologies 1280 infinity II high-performance chromatography (HPLC) equipped with 1200 infinity photodiode array detector (PDA), online degasser, and quaternary pump with a maximum pressure of 400 bar was used for separation of different functionalized or size carbon dots. Particles were separated on Agilent C18 (4.6 × 100 mm, 3.5 μm) column by isocratic elution using ACN: H2O (50:50) and MeOH: H2O (50:50) as mobile phase with flow rate 1 ml min−1. UV/Vis spectra at certain retention time were recorded in the range 200–800 nm by PDA.

In a quartz cell, 1.75 mL citrate buffer at pH 5, 0.5 ml of 100 μg mL−1 MnO4 −, and 0.75 ml of 300 μg mL−1 CQDs were mixed, and the emission at 450 nm (excited at 350 nm) was recorded (F0). Then, 1.5 mL citrate buffer at pH 5 and 0.25 mL As3+ (0.1–2 μg mL−1) were mixed with 0.5 mL of 100 μg mL−1 MnO4 − at pH 5 in a quartz cell. After 12 min 0.75 mL of 300 μg mL−1 CQDs was added, and the fluorescence was recorded (F). ΔF (F-F0) versus As3+ concentration was plotted, and linear range for arsenic detection was reported.

HT-29 cells were seeded into 96 well culture plates (1 × 105 cell) and incubated at 37 °C under CO2 (5%) for 24 h. Then, HT-29 cells were incubated with 1000 μg mL−1 CQDs at 37 °C for 4 h. After incubation, the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) at pH 7.4. Finally, they were observed under the fluorescence microscope using 480 nm excitation filter.

MTT assay was used to test cytotoxicity of the prepared CQDs towards HT-29 cells. Cells were seeded into 96-well plates at the seeding density of 1 × 105 [25]. After incubation for 24 h, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing CQDs at concentrations between 5 and 1000 μg mL−1 for 48 h. Media was removed, and cells were incubated with MTT solution (5 mg/mL in PBS) at 37 °C for four hours. Finally, DMSO (150 μL) was added in each well and shaken for 20 min. The absorbance of each plate was read at 570 nm using ELISA plate reader. Cellular viability was determined by sample and control absorbance using Eq. 2, and the half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) was also calculated as cytotoxicity index.
(2)
c e l l
v i a b i l i t y % =
A
s a m p l e
A
C o n t r o l
100

Optimization of the synthesis process is usually performed to improve quality of the products with the minimum amount of starting material and higher speed [26]. Here, Taguchi orthogonal array that is usually used in quality control is selected to optimize the parameters and identify the most important factors in microwave synthesis of CQDs from quince fruit. Table S1 and Fig. S1 (ESI) identified that 0.1 g of the powder is enough for CQDs synthesis under microwave irradiation with the program shown in Fig. S2 (ESI). Table S2 (ESI) that is the response table for the signal to noise ratio shows that the most important parameters in the synthesis are temperature and the weight of quince fruit powder, respectively. Time of reaction and microwave power have less impact on the synthesis. The temperature in closed microwave system depends on the solvent. Water at 700 W adsorbed about 98% of microwave power and had a rapid increase in temperature [27]. CQDs was not produced when 0.1 g quince powder was hydrothermally heated for 30 min at 200 °C, maximum tolerable temperature of the autoclave Teflon-linen. Therefore, the synthesis of CQDs is faster than traditional hydrothermal methods and the amount of quince fruit powder is smaller.

Morphology of particles was identified by TEM and AFM. As Fig. 1 illustrates, fine uniform particles, below 10 nm are synthesized by microwave irradiation. Fig. S3 (see ESI) shows AFM with higher magnification for the CQDs. In AFM, the shape of the tip can have a drastic effect on the acquired images. If the tip is well characterized, the image can be better interpreted. Two parameters used to model tip geometry are the cone angle of the main pyramid and the radius of the tip end (R). The width of the particles on the surface depends on these two parameters. The relationship between the observed width (W) of a feature and the diameter of the tip (2R), when tip radius is much higher than the particle diameter, can be determined from Eq. (3), where d is the diameter of the particles on the surface.
(3)
d =
W 2
8 R

Therefore, the size of a 20 nm particle, in AFM image recording by a cantilever with a tip diameter of 10 nm, is 5 nm. Considering this equation size distribution of CQDs from Fig. 1a was calculated and mean diameters of the prepared particles were 4.85 ± 0.07 nm (Fig. S4; ESI). These data were confirmed by PSA (Fig. 1c).

Particles prepared by hydrothermal methods compared to those obtained using microwaves irradiations have large size and more thickness, (Fig. S5), are more sticky, and aggregate while dried on the mica surface.

Quince contains organic compounds mainly quinic acid, malic acid, ascorbic acid, pectin, and polyphenols [28]. These infer carboxylic acids functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots. FTIR of the prepared CQDs (Fig. 2 a) identifies surface functional groups. FTIR bands between 3400 and 2800 C m−1 are due to OH stretching, CC of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (characterized peaks of CQDs), and CH2 and CH3 vibrations [29]. Sharp peaks at 1715 cm−1 and 1649 cm−1 are related to stretching vibration of carboxylic and amid CO, and CC skeletal ring. Other bands at 1190–1200 and 1081 cm−1 are related to CO of carboxylic groups, respectively. A band at 1400 cm−1 is due to CO or CN (presence of amine groups) and –COO asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration [18,20]. Fig. 2b shows a typical broad XRD peak at about 22° (2θ) and indicates that amorphous CQD is prepared [18,20].

Surface elements and functional groups were also identified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) evaluation of microwave synthesized CQDs. XPS data showed that the main elements are C (67.9%), O (30.91%), and a small amount of N (0.76%), and S (0.41%). In de-convoluted spectra for C1s, shown in Fig. 2c peaks at 284.6, 286.2, and 288.1 eV are assigned to CC, CC, CO, CN, OCO [15,30]. Fig. 2d demonstrated peaks at 531.1, 532.11, and 534 eV corresponding to CO, CO/CN, and OCO, consequently. Fig. 2e illustrates surface amine/amide or pyrolitic moieties NC (400.1 eV), and pyridinic oxide (405 eV). De-convolutes S2p at 167.98 and 169.18 are due to the presence of metal sulfate. These data are consistent with FTIR data.

The zeta potential of microwave CQDs was recorded by Malvern Zeta Sizer. Data in Fig S6 (see ESI) identified that the particles are negatively charged with a zeta potential of -13.3. Having considered FTIR results, this negative charge is due to carboxylic groups on the surface. High zeta potential confirms a high degree of carboxylic functional groups.

UV/Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 3 a showed absorption bond of CQDs below 240 nm and a broad peak having maximum absorption at 284 nm due to n-π* and π-π* transitions of CO bonds of the carboxylic group and CC sp2 aromatic bond of carbon core within CQDs [18,20]. As prepared CQDs is highly water soluble and emit blue light under UV cabinet (360 nm). Inset of Fig. 3a shows the camera image of the solution under UV and daylight.

Mechanism of photoluminescence (PL) is due to surface energy traps and the quantum effect [1,12,31,32]. The quantum effect is related to particle size and causes blue emission. CQDs surface functional groups introduce different energy levels and consequently a red shift in emission wavelength with observable changes in excitation wavelength. PL characteristics of the prepared CQDs prepared by microwave and conventional hydrothermal methods showed excitation-dependent PL (Fig. 3 c, d and Fig. S7). It was reported [33] that excitation-dependent emission is due to different energy levels incorporated into the CQDs by different surface groups such as CO, CO, OCOH. Passivation of the CQDs reduced the energy levels, and mono-color particles were produced. Concerning FTIR results mentioned above, carboxylic and carbonyl as well as amide groups are present in the particle and are responsible for different functional groups on the prepared CQDs. Emission dependent excitation patterns of the CQDs (Fig. 3c) showed an increase in emission intensity at 450 nm while excited in the range of 310–350 nm. Emission intensity at 450 nm is approximately the same for 350 and 360 nm excitation and decreased while excited at 370-390 nm. This observation confirms that PL is originated from quantum confinement effect. A decrease in emission intensity as well as the red shift in emission wavelength, is observed with excitation from 400 to 500 nm. The red shift reveals that PL properties are also due to surface functional groups that incorporate different surface states [18]. Emission profile of CQDs prepared by hydrothermal methods (Fig. S7) is a bit different with those obtained through microwave irradiations. In Fig. S7 emission intensity at 450 nm increase with an increase in excitation wavelength from 320 to 390 nm. Emission intensity decreased, and red-shifted from 400 to 500 nm excitation. It shows the method of preparation slightly change the particles specifications. Therefore, both surface fluorophor and size of carbon core is responsible for PL of as prepared CQDs by both methods.

The fluorescence images presented in Fig. 4 show the multicolor nature of the prepared CQDs under different excitation filters mentioned in the figure caption. The difference in the appearance of the particles prepared by each method is illustrated from camera photo of the CQDs deposited on the lam. Sticky nature of the particles produced by the hydrothermal heating (Fig. 4) may be related to the presence of pectin in the final particles. These observations identified that PL behavior of CQDs strongly depends on synthetic conditions and especially temperature [12]. Taguchi optimization results (Table S2; see ESI) also explained that temperature is the most important parameter. Quantum yield for CQDs is 8.55%.

CQDs were stable for more than two months while stored at 4 °C with less than 5% loss in intensity. Fig. S8 demonstrates that photo stability and stability over time for CQDs prepared by both preparation protocols are considerable. Photo stability was determined by 60 min continuous irradiation under Xe lamp at 350 nm. PL of the CQDs are also stable in the pH range 4–8 (Fig. S9a). At more acidic solution (less than pH = 4) protonation of the functional groups change the PL intensity [20]. PL intensity was stable in the presence of sodium nitrate.
Stability and multicolor features of these particles infer their use in biological and cell imaging as well as in sensing devices.

FTIR, UV/Vis spectrum and PL properties of the prepared particles identified different functional groups on the particles. Particles with various surface groups can be separated by HPLC columns and then evaluated via UV/Vis spectra recorded by PDA. Therefore, HPLC/PDA was used to realize the origin of difference in fluorescent CQDs. Considering the polar nature of the CQDs, two mobile phases, namely, MeOH: H2O and ACN: H2O were selected for the evaluations. UV/Vis spectra were recorded in the range 200–800 nm. Chromatograms recorded at 254 nm (Fig. 5 ) reveal CQDs with different functional groups. UV/Vis spectrum at peaks retention times on the right side of the figure confirms the differences. Chromatogram for CQDs prepared through the conventional hydrothermal method in Fig. S10 (ESI) indicates three peaks in both mobile phase. Two out of three have the same functional groups (equal UV/Vis spectra). These observations implied that the reaction conditions affect the surface functional groups and passivation of the synthesized CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs in As3+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
Quenching effect of the compound on fluorescence of CQDs is the basis for their use as a sensing device. However, the effect of different cations and anions on PL quenching of the prepared CQDs were tested (Fig. S11 (ESI)). As the results show, Fe3+ and MnO4 − have an intense effect on the PL. It was reported that oxidants such as KMnO4 could inject holes into the CQDs [12]. This process increases the population of the holes in the CQDs and accelerates the electron–hole annihilation, resulting in energy release in the form of chemiluminescence (CL) emission and quenching fluorescence at the CQDs maximum emission wavelength. Permanganate can also oxidize As3+ at different pH [34–36]. Having considered this phenomenon and the possible reaction of As3+ with permanganate ion, the present CQDs were evaluated for possible sensing of As3+. The effect of co-cations on As (III) detection was evaluated, and the data is presented in Fig. S9b. As the selectivity data in Fig. S9b (ESI) shows, Fe3+ interfere seriously in the analysis of arsenic because of its quenching effect on CQDs PL (Fig. S11a). The interference effect was eliminated by adding 0.01 M ethylene di-amine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) before addition of CQDs. Redox reaction of the ions with permanganate are pH dependent. Therefore, at optimum pH obtained for As3+ determination no reaction happens for other cations and no interferences were observed. Arsenic reaction with permanganate ion is pH dependent (Eqs. (3) and (4)). However, pH has a great effect on this reaction and the kind of products. CQDs fluorescence is stable at pH above 4 (Fig. S9a). Quenching PL of CQDs at 450 nm in the presence of permanganate is fast.
(4) 5As3+ + 2MnO4 − + 16H+ → 5As5+ + 2 Mn2+ + 8H2O
(5) 5As3+ + 2MnO4 − + 8H2O → 5As5+ + 2Mn (OH)2 + 12OH−

If As3+ has been added to permanganate ion before mixing with CQDs, it converts to Mn2+ and as a result, an increase in fluorescence of CQDs compared to CQDs-MnO4 − is observed (Fig. S9b). This increase is proportional to arsenic concentration. The effect of parameters such as pH, CQDs and permanganate ion concentration as well as the reaction time between two ions were optimized (Fig. S12). As3+ was detected in linear range 0.1–2 μg mL-1 at pH 5 using citrate buffer. At higher pH (>7) formation of MnO2 (Eq. (3)) cause variation in PL intensity as well as the decrease in reaction rate. The figure of merits of analysis is presented in Table1 (Fig. S13). To overcome the matrix effect, calibration curve in arsenic-free tap water was obtained. Table 1 lists figure of merit for the matrix calibration curve. Tap water spiked with different amount of As3+ was analyzed by both calibrations (Table 2 ). Reliable results were obtained by the matrix calibration curve. External calibration curve has shown recoveries in the range of 79–84% while those of the matrix calibration curve are in the range 96-97.5%.

As it is illustrated in Fig. 6 a, CQDs penetrate HT-29 cells in 4 h, and the cells are observed under invert fluorescence microscope with an excitation filter of 488 nm and emission filter at 518 nm. CQDs’ concentration used in this step was 1000 μg mL−1. It identifies the potential application of the CQDs in cellular imaging and drug delivery.

The low toxicity of CQDs is the most important feature for its biomedical and clinical applications. Cytotoxicity of the CQDs (prepared by microwave irradiation) was tested by MTT assay. Results are demonstrated in Fig. 6b. The IC50% value for HT-29 was calculated as 924.25 μg mL−1. Therefore, it is not toxic to the cells at concentrations lower that IC50%. Hence, it can be used in cell imaging and drug delivery.

It was concluded that temperature is the main factor in the synthesis of CQDs using quince fruit powder as a candidate for natural carbon precursor. The synthetic protocol is green and fast. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time that the surface state is studied by HPLC/PDA. HPLC evaluations, as well as optical properties, showed that the method of CQDs preparations effect on the final particles surfaces functional groups. Microwave irradiations produce well-dispersed fine dots. The multi-color CQDs have long-term stability and low toxicity. Therefore, they can be used for a vast variety of applications such as biomedical imaging, sensing technology, and drug delivery. The prepared CQDs was used as fluorescence probe for As3+ detection and determination.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support of research deputy of Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences under grant number GP95089 is acknowledged. We thank Miss Handali and Miss Kiani for their help in cellular uptake experiments. This paper is extracted from Miss Qorbanpour Pharm D dissertation.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2018.04.006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescent carbon nanodots: synthesis, physicochemical properties and analytical applications
Mater. Today
